title: Chapter Four: The Scripture of Overcoming
content:
> *From the Tyrant Sword Sect intelligence report, stamped CLASSIFIED: "The Jeong estate in Xinye County serves as a satellite of the Southern Edge Sect, contributing annual tithes in exchange for the sect's protection. Current garrison: one family head of moderate skill (estimated second-rate), fifteen trained family members (estimated third-rate), thirty servants (non-combatant). Defensive value: negligible. Strategic value: the estate sits on a ley-line confluence that produces exceptional ambient qi density. Recommend seizure during the Southern Edge delegation's visit, when the sect's attention is directed elsewhere."*

---

He closed his eyes and went inward.

Meditation had always been different for Yeon-shin than it was for others. Where most practitioners described the process as emptying the mind, Yeon-shin experienced it as sharpening the mind to such a fine point that it could perceive the interior landscape of his own body. Meridians became rivers. The dantian became a lake. The qi that flowed through him became visible -- not to his eyes, but to something deeper, a sense that had no name in any text he had read.

*The principles of circulating true energy through the small and large channels are clear to me,* he thought. *The Dynamic Technique established the pathways. Now I need to awaken what flows through them into something more.*

He breathed in. Natural energy entered through his lungs, his pores, his Baihui acupoint at the crown of his head --

He stopped.

The Baihui. The gate at the top of his skull.

For three years, he had trained without examining himself. The Dynamic Technique was an external practice -- it strengthened the body, compressed the meridians, built the vessel. He had not turned his awareness inward, toward the deeper structures of his cultivation, since he had begun.

Now, sitting in silence for the first time in a year, he looked.

What he found made his breath catch.

The Baihui acupoint was open. Not slightly open, not partially open. Open. A gateway the width of a fist, gaping at the crown of his skull, pouring natural energy into his upper dantian with the force of a river through a broken levee. The energy rushed downward, flooding the seat of his consciousness, overwhelming the delicate structures of his spirit with raw, unrefined power.

*This is wrong.* The realization formed slowly, each word carrying the weight of dread. *This has been getting worse. The last time I checked -- a year ago, more -- it was wide, but not like this. It has grown.*

He probed deeper, measuring the rate of expansion against his memory of what the acupoint had been. The calculations were rough. He was not a physician. But the math was not complicated.

*At this rate, I have less than five years before the upper dantian is overwhelmed.*

Five years. Before the energy of heaven and earth dissolved his consciousness like salt in water. Before he became, in the clinical terminology of traditional medicine, an ascendant -- a person whose spirit had been erased by the very energy that sustained it.

*I'm going to die.*

The thought was surreal. He was fifteen years old. His body was the strongest it had ever been. He had just created a new sword technique the night before, had just glimpsed the next stage of his cultivation, had just begun to understand what internal energy truly was.

And none of it mattered, because his skull was a broken vessel, and the water was pouring in.

*Die? Me?*

He sat with the knowledge for a long time. Minutes. Perhaps longer. The meditation posture that had always felt natural now felt like a cage -- his own body, the vessel he had spent three years perfecting, revealed as a flawed container with a crack in the lid.

*What does strength mean,* he thought, *if the strong thing is already breaking?*

This was the question that the martial world never asked. The sects, the clans, the nine orthodox and thirteen demon organizations -- they all assumed that power was cumulative. You trained, you grew stronger, you accumulated decades of cultivation, and eventually you became an elder, a master, a legend. The entire structure of the martial world was built on the premise that time was an ally. That strength and longevity were the same thing.

For Jeong Yeon-shin, they were opposites.

Every moment of cultivation, every breath of natural energy drawn into his body, every act of training that made him stronger also widened the gate in his skull. The very thing that made him a genius -- the open Baihui acupoint, the extraordinary sensitivity to qi that allowed him to perceive energy flows and create martial arts instinctively -- was the thing that was killing him. His greatest strength was not separable from his greatest vulnerability. They were the same phenomenon, viewed from different angles.

*If I stop cultivating, I slow the process. But I remain weak, and weakness in the martial world is its own death sentence.*

*If I continue cultivating, I accelerate the opening. I grow stronger, and I die faster.*

*If I do nothing at all, the gate continues to widen on its own. The natural energy of heaven and earth does not require my cooperation. It pours in regardless.*

There was no correct answer. Every path led to the same destination. The only variable was how much he could accomplish before he arrived there.

*Less than five years.*

The number settled into his bones like cold water. It was not abstract. It was not philosophical. It was a countdown, and it had already been running for fifteen years without his knowledge.

He thought about his brothers' mockery. *Do you think you have the qualities of a great master?* They had meant it as an insult. They had not known -- could not have known -- that the question was not about ambition but about time. A great master needed decades to build a legacy. Yeon-shin had less than five years to build anything at all.

*What can a man accomplish in five years?*

He did not know. But the question itself was clarifying, in the way that the sight of a cliff edge is clarifying. It eliminated distraction. It burned away everything that was not essential.

*I will not spend what remains of my life being pitied. I will not spend it being afraid. I will spend it becoming strong enough to change the terms.*

*There must be a way. The elf doctor who examined me at birth -- he said "unless he awakens on his own." That implies awakening is possible. That implies the condition is not absolute.*

*And the World Tree. The fruit of the World Tree, which the elves guard. It grants life.*

*I need that fruit.*

The resolution hardened inside him like cooling iron. Not hope -- hope was too fragile for what he needed. Something more functional. A decision. A direction.

He uncrossed his legs. His hands trembled as he reached for the door handle. He needed air. He needed to move. He needed to do something other than sit in a dark room with the knowledge of his own expiration date echoing in his skull.

He opened the door.

The smell hit him first. Copper and iron and something organic beneath it, something that bypassed thought and triggered instinct. He had watched hunters butcher deer. He had seen animals bled and deboned. The smell was similar, but there was more of it. Much more.

The estate was silent.

Yeon-shin picked up his sword.

---

He walked through the training ground, across the eastern courtyard, past the servants' quarters. Nothing moved. The night air was thick with the smell of blood, but he saw no bodies, no signs of struggle. It was as if the violence had occurred in perfect silence, or as if the victims had been unable to cry out.

*The Blood Technique of the unorthodox sects,* he thought. *I have only read about it. A method of killing that suppresses sound, leaving the dead where they fall without alerting those nearby.*

His grip on the sword tightened. The Dynamic Technique was already active, energy flowing through his meridians, sharpening his senses. He could feel the residue of foreign qi in the air -- dark, oily, carrying the taste of violence.

He reached the courtyard of the main building.

He saw the bodies.

There were a dozen, perhaps more. Family members, servants, guards. They lay in the positions where they had fallen -- some curled, some sprawled, some still sitting as if they had simply stopped living in the middle of whatever they were doing. Each one bore an identical wound: a single sword cut to the chest, delivered with the mechanical precision of a man practicing forms on a training dummy.

The blood had not pooled in the way stories described. It had soaked into the packed earth or trickled down the stone thresholds of the annex buildings, thin rivulets reflecting the faint moonlight.

Yeon-shin looked at the dead. He recognized faces. A cousin. Two servants who had brought him meals. The wife of one of his half-brothers.

He felt nothing. Or rather, he felt everything -- horror, grief, rage, the visceral revulsion of a boy seeing violent death for the first time -- and his mind, honed by three years of the Dynamic Technique's meditative focus, filed these emotions away in a place where they could not interfere with what needed to happen next.

*Later,* he told the grief. *You can have me later.*

An explosion of sound came from beyond the main gate -- the clash of steel, the crack of qi-enhanced impacts, the particular resonance of two high-level martial artists trying to destroy each other. The Southern Edge masters. They were fighting something outside the walls.

Yeon-shin walked toward the main building. Ten men sat in the courtyard, arranged in a loose semicircle, weapons resting across their knees or leaning against the wall behind them. They had the look of mercenaries -- mismatched armor, varied weapons, the casual alertness of men who killed for a living and found the killing unremarkable.

One of them noticed the boy.

"Who are you?" The speaker wore two swords crossed on his back in the style of the northern unorthodox sects. He was large, scarred, and moved with the economy of a trained fighter as he rose to his feet.

"The third son of this household."

The man did not draw his swords. He walked toward Yeon-shin with heavy steps, the kind of deliberate approach that said, *I do not consider you worth the effort of a weapon.*

He struck downward with an open palm. The blow was fast -- faster than a civilian would have seen -- and carried enough internal energy to shatter bone.

Yeon-shin's eyes flashed blue.

The color was not metaphorical. For an instant, his irises flickered with a pale, sky-blue luminescence, and in that instant the flow of the man's energy became visible to him -- not as an abstraction, but as a physical phenomenon, like watching ink diffuse through water. He could see the qi gathering in the man's shoulder, flowing down through the arm, concentrating in the palm.

He could see where the strike would land before it arrived.

His left hand caught the man's wrist. His right hand struck the back of the trapped fist. He twisted.

The movement was grappling -- a martial art he had never formally studied. But the principles were the principles: leverage, timing, the application of force at the point of greatest mechanical advantage. The Dynamic Technique had given him a body that understood these principles the way a river understands gravity.

He squeezed.

The sound of large bones breaking was sharp and definitive, like a branch snapping under weight. The man screamed. The other nine turned as one.

"Let go!"

"You bastard!"

Yeon-shin released the wrist. The twin-sword fighter staggered backward, cradling his shattered hand, his face contorted with pain and something worse than pain: the humiliation of being subdued by a child.

The man's good hand clenched into a fist. His face went crimson. He lunged forward with a roar, throwing a punch that expanded as it traveled -- a fist technique that used internal energy to magnify the striking surface. For an instant, the fist looked as large as a cauldron lid.

Blue lightning flickered in Yeon-shin's eyes again.

*Head.* The trajectory was obvious. The man was aiming high, a killing blow, but the telegraph was as clear as a signal fire.

The energy of the Dynamic Technique surged through his body. And in the moment of that surge, something happened that he had not planned, had not anticipated, and could not have predicted.

An insight struck him. Not gradually, not through accumulation, but all at once, like a door being kicked open. The Dynamic Technique had been building toward this. Three years of tempering the vessel, three years of training the meridians, three years of learning to move energy through his body while in motion -- all of it had been preparation for this single moment of understanding.

*Intention.*

When the intention to move internal energy becomes strong enough, the energy responds. It does not simply flow through prescribed channels. It goes where the mind commands. It becomes an extension of will.

The realization crystallized into words -- specific, precise words that blazed through his consciousness like fire through dry grass. An oral formula. The compressed essence of a martial art, distilled into language that the body could understand.

*Scripture of Overcoming the Origin.*

The name arrived without deliberation. It was not a name he chose. It was a name that already existed, waiting for him to discover it, the way a statue exists inside a block of marble before the sculptor touches it.

The Scripture of Overcoming the Origin. A true energy circulation method that broke through the limits of the current vessel by redistributing internal energy according to the practitioner's will.

The true energy in his right foot and left hand shifted. It flowed across his body to his right hand and left foot, concentrating, layering, doubling in density. His left foot shattered the packed earth as he launched forward. The iron sword left its scabbard following the swift-sword principle he had discovered the night before.

The blade caught moonlight for a fraction of an instant.

Then it caught three throats.

The twin-sword fighter and the two men flanking him fell simultaneously. Their heads separated from their bodies in a single arc of steel, the cut so clean that the expressions on their faces -- shock, in all three cases, their eyes having followed the blade but their bodies unable to respond -- remained fixed even as they tumbled.

Yeon-shin did not stop. His body was moving before his conscious mind had processed the kill. He twisted, pivoted on his front foot, and drove the sword forward in a straight thrust that pierced the throat of a red-haired woman who had been approaching from behind the fallen men. She had been raising a crescent-shaped weapon. She never completed the motion.

He withdrew the blade. The woman fell forward.

Four dead. Six remained.

His breathing had not changed.

---

The shock of what he had done registered somewhere in the back of his mind, a muffled impact against walls he had built without knowing he was building them. He had just killed four people. He had done it in the span of two heartbeats. He felt nothing about it, and the feeling of feeling nothing was itself a kind of feeling -- a cold, clinical awareness that he had crossed a threshold from which there was no return.

*Later,* he told the horror. *You can have me later.*

A thin-bodied swordsman was staring at him with an expression that had passed through surprise and arrived at something close to calculation. The massive man beside him -- a figure with the body of a bear and the eyes of a fox -- placed a hand on the thin man's shoulder and rose to his feet.

"You have devoted your life to the swift sword," the large man said. His voice was calm, analytical, the voice of a professional assessing another professional's work. "You have no other tricks. Those men were caught off guard. You are a well-trained young swordsman. Nothing more."

He was not entirely wrong. And Yeon-shin, standing over four corpses with an iron sword that was already beginning to feel inadequate in his hand, knew it. The Scripture of Overcoming the Origin had given him a burst of power, but the insight was new and unrefined. Against a true master, it would not be enough.

"Still," the large man continued, "it is truly amazing. How a boy your age can do what you just did is beyond my understanding."

"Blood Flame Sect?" Yeon-shin asked, his eyes on the dead woman's red hair and martial uniform.

"No. I am from the Blade Sect."

The Blade Sect. One of the Thirteen Demons -- the great unorthodox sects whose power rivaled the nine orthodox sects. The name settled in Yeon-shin's mind like a stone dropped into still water, sending ripples of implication outward.

"Two of the Thirteen Demons in a place like Xinye County?" he said. "What business do you have here?"

"It is none of your business."

"Then why are you answering my questions?"

"Because it is a waste." The man smiled. It was the smile of a predator admiring prey that had shown unexpected teeth. "A martial artist as capable as you, at your age, is rare. It amuses me to see a boy from the countryside speak as though he understands the world."

A sneer formed on Yeon-shin's lips. His heart was beating so hard he could feel it in his throat, and every instinct in his body was screaming at him to run. He did not run. Running would mean dying.

"You are probably just afraid of my maternal family," he said. "You rootless bastard of the unorthodox sects."

The words were a gamble. Everything about this moment was a gamble. But Yeon-shin had noticed something when the fight began: the twin-sword fighter had attacked with his bare hand, not his weapons. He had not drawn his swords against a boy of fifteen. Which meant one of two things -- either the man had been contemptuous of the threat, or he had been ordered not to kill.

If it was the latter, then someone in this group knew about Yeon-shin's connection to the Desolate Sect. And if they knew, they were afraid.

"You crazy little brat." The large man's voice dropped into a register that was more growl than speech. The sound vibrated in Yeon-shin's chest like a war drum.

"You cannot kill me," Yeon-shin said. His face was calm. His hands were not. "No matter what I do, it makes no difference to you. I am protected by a name you dare not provoke."

The standoff lasted three breaths.

Then the main gate exploded.

---

The door panels burst inward in a shower of wood and iron. A body rolled through the debris -- a man in a torn martial uniform, bloodied, gasping, his sword still clutched in one hand. Yeon-shin recognized him. Precipice Sword, one of the Southern Edge masters who had accompanied Yeo Il-shin.

A moment later, the wall beside the gate collapsed. Yeo Il-shin and the third Southern Edge disciple tumbled through the gap, driven backward by a force that Yeon-shin could feel before he could see it. Both were wounded. Yeo Il-shin's face was streaked with blood, and her left ear -- her long, elegant, elven left ear -- had been cut away above the point. The wound was raw and terrible against her golden hair.

*I thought she was a sword immortal,* Yeon-shin thought numbly. *I thought nothing in the world could touch her.*

Heavy footsteps. A shadow filled the broken gateway.

The man who walked through the ruins of the Jeong estate's wall was not the largest person Yeon-shin had ever seen, but he was the most present. He occupied space the way a mountain occupies a valley -- immovably, completely, as if the world had been built around him rather than the other way around. He carried a sword that was nearly as tall as Yeon-shin himself, and from its edge, something rippled. Not light. Not shadow. Something that Yeon-shin's newly awakened perception could only describe as intent -- the concentrated desire to destroy, made visible, bleeding from the steel like smoke from a forge.

Every nerve in Yeon-shin's body screamed danger. This was not the reaction of a trained martial artist assessing a superior opponent. This was the reaction of an animal in the presence of a predator so far beyond its capacity that the only reasonable response was paralysis.

The Blade Sect warriors dropped to their knees. The large man who had been speaking to Yeon-shin pressed his forehead to the ground.

"Sect Leader!"

*The leader of the Blade Sect. In person.*

Yeon-shin understood. The weight of presence, the shattered Southern Edge masters, the casual annihilation of the estate's defenders -- all of it made sense now. This was one of the rulers of the Thirteen Demons. A man whose name was spoken in the same breath as the leaders of the nine orthodox sects, not with reverence, but with the particular respect that living things accord to natural disasters.

The Blade Sect Leader's gaze swept the courtyard. It passed over the kneeling warriors, the broken gate, the scattered bodies. It found Yeon-shin.

The boy moved.

Not away. Not toward the exit. He sprinted forward, toward the large man who was still prostrate before his leader. The Blade Sect warrior who had been calculating the value of his life three seconds ago.

The man looked up. His face had time to register surprise.

Yeon-shin's sword entered his back and exited through his abdomen.

The boy placed one foot on the dying man's spine, pulled the blade free, and looked directly at the Blade Sect Leader.

"Even now," he said. Blue light flickered in his eyes, faint but unmistakable. His voice did not shake.

His voice should have shaken. He was fifteen years old, standing in his family's ruined courtyard, ankle-deep in blood that belonged to people he had known since childhood. He had killed five people in the span of minutes. He had discovered that he was dying. And now he was staring down the most dangerous man he had ever encountered, armed with nothing but an iron practice sword and a bluff built on a family connection he was not certain would protect him.

"Can you kill me?"

---
